h2h_summary: Iraq and Norway have never previously met in international football, making their World Cup 2026 Group I clash a historic first encounter.
wc_history_home: Iraq made their only previous World Cup appearance in 1986 in Mexico, finishing bottom of their group with three losses and a single goal to their name — then qualified again 40 years later for 2026.
wc_history_away: Norway are appearing at the World Cup for the first time since France 1998, where they reached the Round of 16 after beating Brazil 2-1 in the group stage, and qualified for 2026 by topping their UEFA group with a perfect record of eight wins from eight.
group_context: Group I is headlined by defending-champion-calibre France and African champions Senegal, with Norway widely tipped for second place thanks to Haaland's firepower; Iraq, the heavy underdogs returning to the World Cup after a 40-year absence, open against Norway in a match that could define their tournament hopes.
strengths_home: Disciplined defensive organisation and hard to break down
Dangerous on counter-attack through direct play to physical strikers
Strong collective unity and tournament experience under Graham Arnold
strengths_away: Erling Haaland — elite finishing and physical dominance in the penalty area
Perfect qualifying record (8W from 8) with 37 goals and just 5 conceded
High-pressing system suffocates opposition build-up and creates turnovers in dangerous areas
team_style_home: Iraq under Graham Arnold deploy a compact 4-5-1 or 4-4-2 block, prioritising defensive organisation and disciplined pressing before transitioning quickly and directly through Aymen Hussein and Ali Al-Hamadi on the counter, with crosses and set pieces as primary attacking weapons.
team_style_away: Norway under Stale Solbakken play an aggressive high-pressing 4-3-3, using explosive wide play to stretch defences and create space for Haaland centrally, with captain Odegaard operating as a creative No. 10 who links midfield to attack.
recent_news: Norway captain Martin Odegaard says he has no concerns about his fitness ahead of his side’s opening Group I match against Iraq. Iraq are back at a World Cup for the first time since 1986, while Norway have ended a 28-year absence stretching back to 1998.
key_players_home: Aymen Hussein (striker, all-time leading scorer with 33 international goals)
Ali Al-Hamadi (forward, former Luton Town / Ipswich Town, first Iraqi in the Premier League)
Amir Al-Ammari (midfielder, Cracovia, scored decisive playoff penalty vs Bolivia)
context: Group I is headlined by defending-champion-calibre France and African champions Senegal, with Norway widely tipped for second place thanks to Haaland's firepower; Iraq, the heavy underdogs returning to the World Cup after a 40-year absence, open against Norway in a match that could define their tournament hopes.
